# **OpenSpec Module: US-Domiciled vs. Irish-Domiciled S\&P 500 FIF & Estate Tax Calculator**

## **1\. Architectural and Domain Specification Context**

The comparative analysis of offshore portfolio investments for New Zealand tax residents requires rigorous, multi-jurisdictional mathematical modeling. As global equity markets become increasingly accessible to retail and institutional investors, the underlying tax and execution frictions associated with differing fund domiciles exert a compounding, non-linear impact on long-term terminal wealth. New Zealand’s Foreign Investment Fund (FIF) tax regime, combined with foreign dividend withholding tax (WHT) applications, micro-frictional brokerage routing costs, and the United States Federal Estate Tax, creates highly divergent outcomes based entirely on fund architecture and internal distribution structures1.  
This OpenSpec architectural and technical specification report defines a new deterministic simulation module for the fif-vs-pie-calculator application, functioning as a definitive source of truth artifact for the openspec/specs directory3. The module is engineered to run an exhaustive long-term simulation comparing a United States-domiciled Distributing S\&P 500 Exchange Traded Fund (ETF) against an Irish-domiciled Accumulating S\&P 500 Undertakings for Collective Investment in Transferable Securities (UCITS) ETF. The specification exhaustively details the mathematical formulas, variable definitions, logic gates, and Behavior-Driven Development (BDD) test scenarios required to model the New Zealand FIF Fair Dividend Rate (FDR) and Comparative Value (CV) taxation methods6.  
Furthermore, the simulation integrates the exact execution friction of Interactive Brokers (IBKR) routing mechanics, differing Foreign Tax Credit (FTC) availability, and the terminal drag of the US Estate Tax applied to Non-Resident Aliens (NRAs)7. By codifying these domain rules into a single Spec-Driven Development (SDD) artifact, the application ensures that all subsequent codebase implementations strictly adhere to the established financial logic, allowing investors to visualize the precise intersection of higher initial expense ratios against long-term structural tax advantages.

## **2\. Regulatory Framework and Tax Domain Logic**

To accurately simulate the long-term wealth trajectory of a New Zealand resident investing in global equities, the calculation engine must dynamically account for intersecting tax treaties, domestic tax loopholes, and structural fund differences.

### **2.1 The New Zealand Foreign Investment Fund (FIF) Regime**

Under New Zealand law, offshore equity investments held by domestic tax residents trigger the Foreign Investment Fund (FIF) tax regime if the total cost basis of those investments exceeds a de minimis threshold of $50,000 NZD at any point during the income year9. The FIF regime is explicitly designed to prevent tax deferral on offshore investments that pay low or zero dividends, effectively ensuring that New Zealand captures tax revenue on presumed global growth12. Once the threshold is breached, investors must calculate their taxable income using one of several approved methods, with the Fair Dividend Rate (FDR) and the Comparative Value (CV) methods being the primary mechanisms utilized by retail investors6.  
The Fair Dividend Rate (FDR) method applies a deemed return of 5% on the opening market value of the qualifying FIF portfolio at the start of the tax year (April 1st for individuals)6. Actual cash dividends received from the offshore asset are treated as excluded income and are not taxed separately under the FDR framework6. This method is highly advantageous during extended bull markets where total returns far exceed 5%, as the investor is effectively shielded from taxation on the excess capital gains. Conversely, if shares are bought and sold within the same tax year, a "quick sale" adjustment must be calculated to capture short-term trading profits1.  
The Comparative Value (CV) method provides a mechanism to tax the actual economic return of the portfolio15. The CV formula calculates taxable income by subtracting the opening market value and any associated costs from the closing market value and any distributions (dividends) received6. A critical statutory feature of the CV method is that if the mathematical result is negative, the taxable FIF income is floored at exactly $0; capital losses cannot be claimed against other domestic income, such as salary or wages6. Individual investors and qualifying family trusts are permitted to evaluate both the FDR and CV methods annually and automatically select the method that yields the lowest taxable income for that specific year10.

### **2.2 The Accumulating CV Tax Arbitrage**

The legislative choice allowing investors to toggle between FDR and CV creates a profound structural arbitrage opportunity when analyzing the domicile and structure of offshore ETFs—specifically Distributing versus Accumulating funds.  
United States-domiciled ETFs, such as those tracking the S\&P 500 under tickers like VOO or SPY, are required by US tax law to distribute cash dividends to shareholders11. During a mild bear market where the capital loss of the asset is less severe than the dividend yield, the total economic return under the CV method remains positive11. Because the CV formula mandates the inclusion of external dividends received, the CV method will generate a small, positive taxable income, subjecting the investor to domestic tax even in a down year.  
Irish-domiciled UCITS ETFs, such as VUAA or CSPX, operate under differing regulatory structures that allow for internal accumulation. These funds automatically reinvest dividends internally, purchasing more underlying shares without distributing cash to the end investor2. Because zero external cash dividends are received by the investor, the "Dividends" variable in the statutory CV formula is strictly zero. Consequently, any capital loss in the underlying asset immediately pushes the CV formula into negative territory2. Because negative CV results are floored at $0, the accumulating structure virtually guarantees that the investor will pay exactly zero New Zealand FIF tax during any year the market posts a negative price return, creating a structural compounding advantage known colloquially as the "CV Accumulating Loophole."

### **2.3 Withholding Tax (WHT) and Foreign Tax Credits (FTC)**

The interaction between cross-border dividend flows and bilateral tax treaties introduces a second layer of complexity to the simulation. US-domiciled ETFs are subject to a 30% statutory withholding tax on dividends paid to foreign investors; however, under the United States-New Zealand Double Tax Agreement, this rate is reduced to 15% upon the successful filing of a W-8BEN form2. This 15% tax is withheld at the investor level. The New Zealand tax system generally allows this withheld amount to be claimed as a Foreign Tax Credit (FTC) to offset the investor's domestic tax liability11. However, the FTC is strictly capped at the lesser of the WHT paid or the New Zealand tax actually payable on that specific FIF income15. In a CV year where the New Zealand tax payable drops below the WHT paid, the excess FTC is permanently lost.  
Irish-domiciled UCITS ETFs benefit from the United States-Ireland tax treaty, which allows the fund to pay a reduced 15% WHT internally on the dividends it receives from the underlying US corporate equities2. This 15% tax is an unrecoverable drag on the fund's Net Asset Value (NAV) and cannot be claimed as an FTC by the New Zealand investor, as the tax was paid by the corporate fund entity, not the individual2. However, because the ETF is structured as accumulating, it distributes no external cash, resulting in a 0% WHT application at the final investor level2.

## **3\. United States Estate Tax Exposure for Non-Resident Aliens**

A critical deficiency in standard long-term wealth simulators is the omission of terminal succession taxes, which can catastrophically erode intergenerational wealth. The United States imposes a Federal Estate Tax on Non-Resident Aliens (NRAs) holding "US-situs" assets upon their death7.  
US-domiciled ETFs, regardless of where the brokerage account is physically located, are legally classified as US-situs assets2. While US citizens and domiciled residents currently enjoy an estate tax exemption threshold exceeding $13.99 million (indexing to roughly $15 million by 2026), the exemption for NRAs has been rigidly fixed at $60,000 USD since 1976 and is not adjusted for inflation16. Upon the death of the NRA investor, any US-situs portfolio value exceeding this $60,000 USD threshold is subjected to a progressive tax scale ranging from 18% to 40%2. For large portfolios, the top marginal rate of 40% effectively functions as a flat tax on the vast majority of the asset base, requiring executors to file IRS Form 706-NA and obtain a Federal Transfer Certificate before assets can be unfrozen and transferred to beneficiaries7.  
Conversely, Irish-domiciled UCITS ETFs are legally classified as non-US-situs assets2. By utilizing an offshore corporate wrapper domiciled in Ireland, the New Zealand investor permanently shields the portfolio from the US Federal Estate Tax2. The terminal simulation module must deduct a 40% liability from the US-domiciled asset balance exceeding the $60,000 threshold, while applying a $0 liability to the Irish-domiciled asset, dramatically shifting the terminal wealth outcomes in favor of the UCITS structure for lifelong buy-and-hold investors.

## **4\. Execution Friction: Interactive Brokers (IBKR) Cost Modeling**

To ensure the simulation accurately reflects real-world portfolio growth, micro-frictions related to currency conversion and trade execution must be mathematically integrated. Interactive Brokers (IBKR) is widely utilized by New Zealand investors for offshore access due to its low margin rates and institutional-grade pricing20.

### **4.1 US Equities Tiered Pricing**

IBKR Pro offers both Fixed and Tiered pricing structures22. For retail investors executing regular, moderate-sized contributions into US-domiciled ETFs, the Tiered pricing model is mathematically optimal22. The Tiered plan charges a base rate of $0.0035 USD per share, coupled with a highly competitive minimum charge of $0.35 USD per order23. The simulation must calculate the theoretical share count based on the trade value and the assumed price of the ETF, applying the volumetric fee and flooring it at the $0.35 minimum.

### **4.2 European Exchange (LSE) Routing**

Accessing Irish-domiciled UCITS ETFs requires routing orders through European exchanges, typically the London Stock Exchange (LSE) using the @LSEETF routing destination in USD24. European exchange fee structures differ significantly from US per-share models, instead utilizing percentage-based commissions24. The simulation models the execution cost at 0.05% of the total trade value24. Furthermore, European exchanges enforce higher minimum commissions compared to domestic US routing. The simulation assumes a hard floor minimum of $2.00 USD per trade for LSE ETF routing24.

### **4.3 Foreign Exchange (FX) Conversion Mechanisms**

New Zealand investors must cross the NZD/USD currency pair to acquire these assets. IBKR offers distinct mechanisms for currency conversion, which the simulation allows the user to toggle:

* **Manual Spot Conversion:** The investor executes a discrete spot currency trade prior to purchasing the equity. This incurs a flat minimum fee of $2.00 USD for smaller retail sums, with minimal spread20.  
* **Auto-Conversion:** When an investor submits a buy order for a USD-denominated asset without sufficient USD cash, IBKR automatically converts the required NZD. This service does not charge a discrete commission minimum but instead applies a hidden spread of 0.03% to the prevailing interbank rate8. For smaller, high-frequency contributions, Auto-Conversion prevents the $2.00 minimum from acting as a massive percentage drag on the capital deployed.

## **5\. Mathematical Specification and State Progression Formulas**

The calculation engine operates utilizing discrete, annualized time steps ![][image1]. All principal calculations for market growth and internal compounding are executed natively in USD to mirror brokerage reality, while FIF tax calculations are executed in NZD based on the prevailing exchange rate for statutory compliance.

### **5.1 State Variables and Constants**

The system relies on the following defined state variables and constants to execute the progression loop:

| Variable | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| **![][image2]** | Annual NZD capital contribution deposited at the start of year ![][image3]. |
| ![][image4] | Exchange rate mapping NZD to USD at year ![][image3] (e.g., 0.60). |
| ![][image5] | Gross S\&P 500 price return index for year ![][image3]. |
| ![][image6] | Annual gross dividend yield of the S\&P 500 index. |
| ![][image7] | The investor's New Zealand marginal tax rate (e.g., 0.33). |
| ![][image8] | The absolute USD value of Asset A (US-Domiciled) at the end of year ![][image3]. |
| ![][image9] | The absolute USD value of Asset B (Irish-Domiciled) at the end of year ![][image3]. |

### **5.2 Foreign Exchange (FX) Friction Engine**

The function ![][image10] is utilized to convert NZD contributions to USD, accounting for the user-selected FX toggle8.

**![][image12]**  
**![][image13]**

### **5.3 Asset A (US-Domiciled Distributing ETF) Simulation**

Asset A represents a highly liquid, US-domiciled S\&P 500 ETF, characterized by extremely low internal costs but higher external tax friction.  
**Parameters:**

* **![][image16]** (0.03% Expense Ratio)  
* ![][image17] (15% investor-level WHT)  
* ![][image18] \= Constant benchmark share price (e.g., $500) utilized purely for volumetric share count calculations.

**Brokerage Function (US Tiered Routing):** Let ![][image19] represent the USD trade value. The theoretical number of shares ![][image20] equals ![][image21].  
![][image22]  
![][image23]

1. **Opening Balance Definition:** ![][image24]  
2. **Add Net Capital Contribution:** ![][image25]  
3. **Apply Market Price Return:** ![][image26]  
4. **Calculate Gross External Dividend:** ![][image27]  
5. **Calculate Withholding Tax:** ![][image28]  
6. **Reinvest Net Dividend:** ![][image29]  
   ![][image30]  
7. **Closing Pre-Tax Balance Definition:** ![][image31]

**FIF Tax Calculation Engine (Asset A):** All values must be translated into NZD at the prevailing exchange rate prior to statutory calculation6.  
![][image32]  
![][image33]  
![][image34]  
![][image35]  
![][image36]  
![][image37]  
**Tax Payable & FTC Application:**  
**![][image38]**  
**![][image39]**  
**![][image40]**  
*To represent the tax drag on the compounding curve, the net NZD tax liability is converted back to USD and subtracted from the portfolio via an automated sell order. This sale incurs a corresponding sell brokerage fee, yielding the final ![][image8] state.*

### **5.4 Asset B (Irish-Domiciled Accumulating ETF) Simulation**

Asset B represents a UCITS-compliant, Irish-domiciled ETF designed to shelter the investor from external cash drag and terminal estate liabilities, at the cost of marginally higher internal expense ratios.  
**Parameters:**

* **![][image41]** (0.07% Expense Ratio)  
* ![][image42] (15% internal fund drag pursuant to the US-Ireland treaty)  
* ![][image43] (0% investor-level WHT due to accumulation)

**Brokerage Function (LSE ETF Routing):**  
**![][image44]**  
**![][image45]**  
**Annual State Progression:**

1. **Opening Balance Definition:** ![][image46]  
2. **Add Net Capital Contribution:** ![][image47]  
3. **Apply Internal Compound Return:** The internal growth mechanism incorporates the S\&P 500 price return, the internal reinvestment of dividends net of the 15% treaty WHT, and the subtraction of the TER2.  
   ![][image48]  
4. **Closing Pre-Tax Balance Definition:** ![][image49]

**FIF Tax Calculation Engine (Asset B):** Converted to NZD for statutory compliance:  
![][image50]  
![][image51]  
![][image35]  
![][image52]  
*Analyst Note: Because the UCITS fund is accumulating, ![][image53] is strictly ![][image54]. Therefore, any market year resulting in ![][image55] mathematically guarantees that ![][image56] will floor at ![][image54].*  
*![][image37]*  
**Tax Payable (No FTC Availability):**  
**![][image57]**  
*Similarly, the net NZD tax is converted back to USD and subtracted from the portfolio via a sell order on the LSE, incurring European brokerage fees to yield the final ![][image9] state.*

### **5.5 Terminal US Estate Tax Calculation**

At simulation year ![][image58], the system halts active compounding and simulates the death of the Non-Resident Alien investor to determine final inherited wealth7.  
**Asset A (Classified as US-Situs):**  
**![][image59]**  
**![][image60]**

## **6\. OpenSpec Requirements Definition**

The following behavioral and architectural requirements define the operational state of the new calculation module. This section serves as the definitive specification for the SDD implementation4.

* REQ-FIF-001: The calculation engine SHALL implement a discrete, year-by-year simulation loop spanning the user-defined investment time horizon.  
* REQ-FIF-002: The system MUST simultaneously support two distinct asset models: A US-domiciled distributing ETF and an Irish-domiciled accumulating UCITS ETF.  
* REQ-FIF-003: The system SHALL process New Zealand Dollar (NZD) contributions and convert them to United States Dollars (USD) utilizing a user-toggled FX fee model (Auto-Conversion vs. Manual Spot).  
* REQ-FIF-004: The system MUST calculate execution brokerage for Asset A using IBKR Pro Tiered routing rules (![][image63] per share, with a hard minimum of ![][image64] per order).  
* REQ-FIF-005: The system MUST calculate execution brokerage for Asset B using European LSE routing rules (![][image65] of total trade value, with a hard minimum of ![][image66] per order).  
* REQ-FIF-006: The system SHALL isolate the 15% WHT for Asset A at the investor distribution level, enabling it to be claimed as a Foreign Tax Credit (FTC) against the New Zealand domestic tax liability.  
* REQ-FIF-007: The system SHALL model the 15% WHT for Asset B as an unrecoverable internal NAV drag, distributing zero external cash and explicitly preventing any FTC claims by the investor.  
* REQ-FIF-008: The system MUST calculate the New Zealand FIF FDR amount as precisely 5% of the opening NZD portfolio value.  
* REQ-FIF-009: The system MUST calculate the New Zealand FIF CV amount as the difference between the closing NZD value and opening NZD value, strictly incorporating external gross dividends received.  
* REQ-FIF-010: The system SHALL determine taxable FIF income by dynamically selecting the lesser of the calculated FDR and CV values on an annual basis.  
* REQ-FIF-011: The system MUST apply a mathematical floor of exactly $0 to the CV value if the output is negative, preventing the recognition of FIF losses.  
* REQ-FIF-012: The system SHALL apply a 40% US Federal Estate Tax on the terminal value of Asset A that exceeds the ![][image67] USD statutory exemption.  
* REQ-FIF-013: The system SHALL apply a 0% US Federal Estate Tax on the terminal value of Asset B, recognizing its non-US-situs classification.

## **7\. Behavior-Driven Development (BDD) Scenarios**

The following Gherkin-formatted scenarios provide deterministic test vectors for the AI implementation agent to validate logic gates, structural tax applications, and mathematical accuracy during test-driven development5.

### **Scenario 1: Auto FX Conversion Fee Application**

* **GIVEN** an annual portfolio contribution of $10,000 NZD.  
* **AND** an exchange rate of 0.60 NZD/USD (yielding a Gross USD conversion of $6,000.00).  
* **WHEN** the user selects the "Auto-Conversion" FX routing toggle.  
* **THEN** the system applies an interbank spread fee of 0.03%.  
* **AND** the absolute FX fee calculated is exactly $1.80 USD.  
* **AND** the net USD available for asset purchase is $5,998.20 USD.

### **Scenario 2: Manual FX Conversion Fee Application**

* **GIVEN** an annual portfolio contribution of $10,000 NZD.  
* **AND** an exchange rate of 0.60 NZD/USD (yielding a Gross USD conversion of $6,000.00).  
* **WHEN** the user selects the "Manual Spot Conversion" FX routing toggle.  
* **THEN** the system applies a flat transaction minimum of $2.00 USD.  
* **AND** the net USD available for asset purchase is $5,998.00 USD.

### **Scenario 3: US Equity Brokerage Friction (Asset A)**

* **GIVEN** a net USD available balance of $5,998.20 USD.  
* **AND** the assumed benchmark share price of Asset A is $500.00.  
* **WHEN** the system calculates the IBKR Tiered purchase brokerage for US equities.  
* **THEN** the nominal share count evaluates to 11.9964 shares.  
* **AND** the pure volumetric fee ($0.0035 \* 11.9964) evaluates to $0.0419 USD.  
* **AND** the system overrides the volumetric fee with the statutory minimum fee of $0.35 USD.  
* **AND** the final invested capital in Asset A is exactly $5,997.85 USD.

### **Scenario 4: LSE Equity Brokerage Friction (Asset B)**

* **GIVEN** a net USD available balance of $5,998.20 USD.  
* **WHEN** the system calculates the LSE ETF purchase brokerage for European routing.  
* **THEN** the pure volumetric fee ($5,998.20 \* 0.0005) evaluates to $2.9991 USD.  
* **AND** because $2.9991 is strictly greater than the $2.00 minimum, the fee applied is $3.00 USD (rounded).  
* **AND** the final invested capital in Asset B is exactly $5,995.20 USD.

### **Scenario 5: FIF FDR Dominance in an Extended Bull Market**

* **GIVEN** an opening Asset A portfolio value of $100,000 NZD.  
* **AND** the index experiences a capital return of 10.0% and yields a gross dividend of 1.5%.  
* **WHEN** the system calculates the competing FIF taxation methods.  
* **THEN** the FDR calculation yields exactly $5,000 NZD.  
* **AND** the CV calculation yields exactly $11,500 NZD.  
* **AND** the system automatically selects the FDR method ($5,000 NZD) as the taxable base to minimize tax liability.

### **Scenario 6: The "CV Accumulating Loophole" in a Mild Bear Market**

This scenario explicitly models the mathematical loophole where a Distributing ETF triggers taxation while an Accumulating ETF legally avoids it during a mild market contraction11.

* **GIVEN** an opening portfolio value of $100,000 NZD for both Asset A and Asset B.  
* **AND** the market index experiences a capital return of \-1.40% and a gross dividend yield of 1.50%.  
* **AND** the New Zealand Marginal Tax Rate is set to 33%.  
* **WHEN** the system evaluates Asset A (Distributing).  
* **THEN** the total CV return remains positive: \-1.40% (capital) \+ 1.50% (dividend) \= \+0.10%.  
* **AND** the CV taxable income evaluates to $100 NZD.  
* **AND** the system calculates a gross New Zealand tax liability of $33.00 NZD.  
* **WHEN** the system evaluates Asset B (Accumulating).  
* **THEN** the internal return evaluates to \-1.40% (capital) \+ \[1.50% \* 0.85\] (net internal dividend) \= \-0.125%.  
* **AND** because the fund is structurally accumulating, external dividends recorded are $0.  
* **AND** the CV taxable income calculates as a negative integer, instantly flooring at exactly $0 NZD.  
* **AND** the system calculates a gross New Zealand tax liability of exactly $0.00 NZD.

### **Scenario 7: Foreign Tax Credit (FTC) Capping Limits**

* **GIVEN** Asset A generates a gross dividend resulting in $1,500 USD.  
* **AND** a 15% WHT of $225 USD is paid at the investor distribution level.  
* **AND** the exchange rate is constant at 0.60 NZD/USD (yielding a $375 NZD equivalent WHT).  
* **WHEN** the New Zealand tax calculated on the FIF income (via FDR) is $1,650 NZD.  
* **THEN** the system claims the full $375 NZD as an FTC, lowering the net tax payable to $1,275 NZD.  
* **WHEN** the New Zealand tax calculated on the FIF income (via CV in a contracted market year) is $100 NZD.  
* **THEN** the system caps the claimable FTC at $100 NZD, reducing the net tax to $0 NZD, and the remaining $275 NZD FTC is permanently lost.

### **Scenario 8: Terminal US Estate Tax Drag Application**

* **GIVEN** the simulation algorithm reaches terminal year ![][image58], indicating the death of the investor.  
* **WHEN** the terminal gross value of Asset A (US-Domiciled) evaluates to $300,000 USD.  
* **THEN** the system identifies the asset as US-Situs.  
* **AND** the system applies a 40% tax to the total value exceeding $60,000 USD.  
* **AND** the calculated US Estate Tax evaluates to $96,000 USD.  
* **AND** the final inherited wealth passed to beneficiaries is $204,000 USD.  
* **WHEN** the terminal gross value of Asset B (Irish-Domiciled) evaluates to $300,000 USD.  
* **THEN** the system identifies the asset as Non-US-Situs.  
* **AND** the calculated US Estate Tax evaluates to exactly $0 USD.  
* **AND** the final inherited wealth passed to beneficiaries is $300,000 USD.

## **8\. Cross-Validation Vectors and Integration Benchmarks**

To ensure the calculation engine implementation is mathematically exact and devoid of floating-point drifts, the following baseline test vector outputs must be flawlessly replicated by the software's unit testing suite prior to production merge.  
**Global Initial Conditions:**

* Initial Capital Allocation \= $100,000 NZD.  
* No subsequent annual contributions (to ensure clean validation of compounding mathematics).  
* FX Rate \= Constant 0.60 NZD/USD.  
* Auto-Conversion FX mode selected (0.03% spread fee).  
* Initial USD Pre-Fee \= $60,000. Net Initial USD \= $59,982.00 USD.  
* NZ Marginal Tax Rate \= 33%.  
* Year 1 Market Return Vector \= \+10% Capital Return, \+1.5% Gross Dividend Yield.

### **8.1 Asset A (VOO) Year 1 Baseline Test Vector**

| Metric | Expected System Output | Domain Notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Opening Value (USD)** | $59,981.58 | Calculated net of the initial $0.42 USD IBKR Tiered purchase minimum fee. |
| **Opening Value (NZD)** | $99,969.30 | Translated statically at the 0.60 exchange rate. |
| **FDR Calculation (NZD)** | $4,998.47 | Represents 5% of the Opening NZD Value. |
| **CV Calculation (NZD)** | $12,740.50 | Reflects the full capture of the 10% capital gain plus the 1.5% external dividend. |
| **Selected FIF (NZD)** | $4,998.47 | The system automatically selects FDR as it represents the lower taxable base. |
| **Gross NZ Tax (NZD)** | $1,649.49 | Represents 33% of the selected FDR amount. |
| **WHT Paid (USD)** | $134.96 | Represents 15% of the $899.72 USD Gross Dividend. |
| **FTC Claimed (NZD)** | $224.93 | WHT mapped back to NZD to act as a credit. |
| **Net NZ Tax Payable (NZD)** | $1,424.56 | Calculated as $1,649.49 minus the $224.93 allowable FTC. |
| **Closing Value (USD)** | $65,871.07 | Final portfolio value post-tax, post-reinvestment, and post-brokerage execution. |

### **8.2 Asset B (VUAA) Year 1 Baseline Test Vector**

| Metric | Expected System Output | Domain Notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Opening Value (USD)** | $59,952.01 | Calculated net of the initial $29.99 USD LSE purchase fee (0.05%). |
| **Opening Value (NZD)** | $99,920.01 | Translated statically at the 0.60 exchange rate. |
| **FDR Calculation (NZD)** | $4,996.00 | Represents 5% of the Opening NZD Value. |
| **CV Calculation (NZD)** | $11,196.04 | 10% gain plus internal 1.275% net dividend minus the 0.07% TER. |
| **Selected FIF (NZD)** | $4,996.00 | The system automatically selects FDR as it represents the lower taxable base. |
| **Gross NZ Tax (NZD)** | $1,648.68 | Represents 33% of the selected FDR amount. |
| **WHT Paid (USD)** | $0.00 | Zero investor-level WHT applied due to the accumulating structure. |
| **FTC Claimed (NZD)** | $0.00 | No FTC allowable against domestic tax. |
| **Net NZ Tax Payable (NZD)** | $1,648.68 | The full amount remains payable in New Zealand without credit offsets. |
| **Closing Value (USD)** | $65,678.42 | Final portfolio value post-tax and post-brokerage execution. |

While Asset A marginally outperforms Asset B in Year 1 due to the utility of the Foreign Tax Credit against the FIF FDR tax11, Asset B's permanent shield against the 40% US Estate Tax and its structural advantage during market contractions (the CV Loophole) creates a dynamic, multi-decade breakeven horizon. The front-end user interface will visualize these intersecting curves to aid in tax-efficient portfolio design.

## **9\. System Variables and Data Dictionary**

To maintain architectural integrity, the simulation calculation engine must strictly adhere to the nomenclature, data types, and constraints specified below.

| Variable Name | Type | Domain / Constraints | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| nzd\_initial\_capital | Float | \> 0.00 | The starting NZD portfolio principal amount. |
| nzd\_annual\_contribution | Float | \>= 0.00 | The end-of-year recurring capital contribution. |
| fx\_mode | String | \["auto", "manual"\] | Toggles the mathematical FX friction modeling. |
| fx\_rate | Float | \> 0.00 | Base scalar assumption for the NZD/USD exchange rate. |
| nz\_tax\_rate | Float | \[0.105, 0.175, 0.28, 0.33, 0.39\] | The user's marginal NZ tax rate or Prescribed Investor Rate (PIR). |
| market\_returns | Array\[Float\] | \[-1.00, 10.00\] | Discrete array of annualized capital returns mapping the simulation horizon. |
| dividend\_yield | Float | \>= 0.00 | The assumed gross dividend yield of the underlying index. |
| asset\_a\_ter | Float | Constant 0.0003 | Total Expense Ratio of the US-Domiciled ETF model. |
| asset\_b\_ter | Float | Constant 0.0007 | Total Expense Ratio of the Irish-Domiciled ETF model. |
| asset\_a\_wht | Float | Constant 0.15 | External WHT scalar applied to cash distributions. |
| asset\_b\_wht | Float | Constant 0.15 | Internal WHT drag scalar on Irish internal accumulation. |

## **10\. Implementation Task Ledger (tasks.md)**

The following sequential implementation tasks define the operational workflow for the AI agent to execute this specification into the live codebase4.

1. **\[ \] Environment Scaffolding:** Generate a new module directory src/simulations/us-vs-irish-fif within the existing fif-vs-pie-calculator architecture.  
2. **\[ \] Data Type Enforcement:** Implement the data dictionary interfaces defined in Section 9 utilizing strictly typed definitions.  
3. **\[ \] FX Friction Engine Construction:** Build the calculate\_fx\_friction() function, mathematically enforcing the $2.00 flat minimum for Manual mode and the 0.03% spread for Auto mode.  
4. **\[ \] Brokerage Routing Engine:** Build the calculate\_ibkr\_brokerage(asset\_type, trade\_value) function. Enforce the $0.0035 per share / $0.35 minimum for Asset A, and the 0.05% volumetric / $2.00 minimum for Asset B.  
5. **\[ \] FIF Tax Engine Sub-Routines:** Construct the calculate\_fif\_and\_tax() function.  
   * *Constraint A:* Ensure Asset A CV logic strictly adds gross dividends to the return calculation.  
   * *Constraint B:* Ensure Asset B CV logic strictly excludes dividends, maintaining them as internally accumulated capital.  
   * *Constraint C:* Floor the CV calculation result at 0.00 for both assets to prevent loss recognition.  
   * *Constraint D:* Implement FTC capping logic, ensuring FTC claimed cannot exceed total NZ tax liability for that interval.  
6. **\[ \] Terminal State Protocol:** Build the calculate\_estate\_tax(portfolio\_value, domicile) function, strictly enforcing the NRA ![][image67] USD threshold and 40% rate exclusively for US-situs classified assets.  
7. **\[ \] BDD Test Integration:** Translate the Gherkin Scenarios outlined in Section 7 and the rigorous Validation Vectors detailed in Section 8 into the repository's native unit testing framework.  
8. **\[ \] Artifact Archival:** Submit the finalized code for architectural review and merge this delta spec into the OpenSpec source\_of\_truth documentation tree4.

#### **Works cited**

1. The Foreign Investment Fund (FIF) Tax Regime in New Zealand \- Creative Planning, [https://creativeplanning.com/international/insights/country-guides/foreign-investment-fund-tax-new-zealand/](https://creativeplanning.com/international/insights/country-guides/foreign-investment-fund-tax-new-zealand/)  
2. Complete Guide to Ireland-Domiciled ETF Investing in Singapore \[2026\] \- StashAway, [https://www.stashaway.sg/r/guide-ireland-domiciled-etfs-singapore](https://www.stashaway.sg/r/guide-ireland-domiciled-etfs-singapore)  
3. ktersius/fif-vs-pie-calculator · GitHub \- GitHub, [https://github.com/ktersius/fif-vs-pie-calculator](https://github.com/ktersius/fif-vs-pie-calculator)  
4. OpenSpec | Spec-Driven Development, [https://intent-driven.dev/knowledge/openspec/](https://intent-driven.dev/knowledge/openspec/)  
5. How Specs Work in OpenSpec \- Rushi's, [https://www.rushis.com/how-specs-work-in-openspec/](https://www.rushis.com/how-specs-work-in-openspec/)  
6. QB 23/10: Foreign investment fund (FIF) calculation methods in cases of non-compliance \- Tax Technical, [https://www.taxtechnical.ird.govt.nz/-/media/project/ir/tt/pdfs/questions-we-ve-been-asked/2023/qb-23-10.pdf?modified=20231219213100](https://www.taxtechnical.ird.govt.nz/-/media/project/ir/tt/pdfs/questions-we-ve-been-asked/2023/qb-23-10.pdf?modified=20231219213100)  
7. US Estate Tax for Non-Residents: Do Your ETFs & Stocks Qualify? | Skybound Wealth, [https://www.skyboundwealth.com/technical-guides/u-s-estate-tax-rules-for-non-residents](https://www.skyboundwealth.com/technical-guides/u-s-estate-tax-rules-for-non-residents)  
8. Commissions & Fees | Interactive Brokers Australia Pty. Ltd., [https://www.interactivebrokers.com.au/en/pricing/commissions-home.php](https://www.interactivebrokers.com.au/en/pricing/commissions-home.php)  
9. Offshore share investments \- KPMG International, [https://assets.kpmg.com/content/dam/kpmg/pdf/2015/07/Offshore-Share-Investments-FIF-2015.pdf](https://assets.kpmg.com/content/dam/kpmg/pdf/2015/07/Offshore-Share-Investments-FIF-2015.pdf)  
10. Income tax – Share investments (whether the FIF rules apply) \- fact sheet, [https://www.taxtechnical.ird.govt.nz/-/media/project/ir/tt/pdfs/fact-sheets/2024/is-24-10-fs-1.pdf?modified=20241218024123](https://www.taxtechnical.ird.govt.nz/-/media/project/ir/tt/pdfs/fact-sheets/2024/is-24-10-fs-1.pdf?modified=20241218024123)  
11. NZ FIF Rules: 2026 Changes, Thresholds, FDR, CV, RAM Methods | Become Wealth's Blog, [https://www.become.nz/articles/fif-rules-nz-overseas-investment-tax-guide](https://www.become.nz/articles/fif-rules-nz-overseas-investment-tax-guide)  
12. Reducing the tax barrier for migrants and returning expats \- Deloitte | tax@hand, [https://www.taxathand.com/article/38512/New-Zealand/2025/Reducing-the-tax-barrier-for-migrants-and-returning-expats](https://www.taxathand.com/article/38512/New-Zealand/2025/Reducing-the-tax-barrier-for-migrants-and-returning-expats)  
13. New tax rules for offshore portfolio investment in shares \- Tax Technical \- Inland Revenue, [https://www.taxtechnical.ird.govt.nz/new-legislation/act-articles/taxation-savings-investment-and-miscellaneous-provisions-act-2006-and-taxation-annual-rates-of-incom/new-tax-rules-for-offshore-portfolio-investment-in-shares](https://www.taxtechnical.ird.govt.nz/new-legislation/act-articles/taxation-savings-investment-and-miscellaneous-provisions-act-2006-and-taxation-annual-rates-of-incom/new-tax-rules-for-offshore-portfolio-investment-in-shares)  
14. Guide to foreign investment funds \- IR461 \- Inland Revenue, [https://www.ird.govt.nz/-/media/project/ir/home/documents/forms-and-guides/ir400---ir499/ir461/ir461.pdf?modified=20260331214052](https://www.ird.govt.nz/-/media/project/ir/home/documents/forms-and-guides/ir400---ir499/ir461/ir461.pdf?modified=20260331214052)  
15. Foreign Investment Funds (FIF) \- Long \+ Cowan Chartered Accountants • Wellington, [https://longcowan.co.nz/foreign-investement-funds-fif/](https://longcowan.co.nz/foreign-investement-funds-fif/)  
16. Dividend Withholding Tax and Estate Tax on US Equities: A Singapore Investor's Guide \[2026\] \- StashAway, [https://www.stashaway.sg/r/dividend-withholding-tax-estate-tax-us-equities-singapore](https://www.stashaway.sg/r/dividend-withholding-tax-estate-tax-us-equities-singapore)  
17. Reporting your overseas income \- Inland Revenue, [https://www.ird.govt.nz/income-tax/income-tax-for-individuals/types-of-individual-income/overseas-income/reporting-your-overseas-income](https://www.ird.govt.nz/income-tax/income-tax-for-individuals/types-of-individual-income/overseas-income/reporting-your-overseas-income)  
18. How Do U.S. Estate Tax Treaties Protect Non-Resident Property Owners?, [https://www.greenbacktaxservices.com/tax-qa/estate-tax-treaty-non-resident-property/](https://www.greenbacktaxservices.com/tax-qa/estate-tax-treaty-non-resident-property/)  
19. US estate tax relief: key considerations for non-US citizens | Brodies LLP, [https://brodies.com/insights/wills-and-estate-planning/us-estate-tax-relief-key-considerations-for-non-us-citizens/](https://brodies.com/insights/wills-and-estate-planning/us-estate-tax-relief-key-considerations-for-non-us-citizens/)  
20. Interactive Brokers Review \- MoneyHub NZ, [https://www.moneyhub.co.nz/interactive-brokers-review.html](https://www.moneyhub.co.nz/interactive-brokers-review.html)  
21. Interactive Brokers NZ Review 2026: 0.002% FX, US$0/Trade — IBKR Pro Fees \- ETFs, [https://etfs.co.nz/platform/interactive-brokers/](https://etfs.co.nz/platform/interactive-brokers/)  
22. IBKR Fixed vs Tiered Pricing: Complete Fee Comparison Guide \- MatchMyBroker, [https://www.matchmybroker.com/articles/ibkr-fixed-vs-tiered-pricing-a-complete-fee-analysis](https://www.matchmybroker.com/articles/ibkr-fixed-vs-tiered-pricing-a-complete-fee-analysis)  
23. Commissions & Fees | Interactive Brokers U.K. Limited, [https://www.interactivebrokers.co.uk/en/pricing/commissions-home.php](https://www.interactivebrokers.co.uk/en/pricing/commissions-home.php)
